title: Outstanding TC Enhancement in 5d-3d Y2NiIrO6 by Compression
authors: Zheng Deng, Yao Zhang, Sijia Zhang, Jing Song, Wanli He, Yuanzhe Li, Meilin Jin, Xiang Li, Guanghua Liu, Zhen Dong, Jinkai Bi, Wenmin Li, Jianfa Zhao, Jun Zhang, Yi Peng, Luchuan Shi, Junling Meng, Xiancheng Wang, Changqing Jin
abstract: Understanding and predicting the properties of 5d compounds critically depend on the identification of the superexchange interactions from which their magnetism emerges. The study of pressure effects on double perovskites Y2NiIrO6 (YNIO) provide deep insight toward this goal. At ambient pressure, YNIO is a ferrimagnetic insulator with the Ir4+-5d Jeff = 1/2 Mott-insulating state. Under the physical pressure up to 17 GPa, the compound exhibits concurrent compression on Ni/Ir-O bond lengths and Ni-O-Ir bond angles, leading to increase of the Curie temperature from 192 to 240 K. In contrary, external pressure increases distanced Ir-Ir interaction and in turn induces magnetic frustration in Sr2IrO4/Sr3Ir2O7 due to the extended 5d orbitals. In YNIO, the rock-salt ordered Ni-Ir naturally blocks extended superexchange beyond the nearest neighbor, and in turn suppresses such magnetic frustration. Moreover, the orthogonal Ni eg-Ir t2g pathway in YNIO is robust under lattice distortion, while the superexchange is weakened by bond bending in La2NiMnO6 with a similar half-filed eg-t2g configuration. Our findings establish a framework for elucidating the mechanism of 5d-3d superexchange and guides bond-engineered magnetism in iridate-related systems.
summary: This study demonstrates that applying physical pressure to the double perovskite Y2NiIrO6 significantly boosts its magnetic ordering temperature. Unlike chemical substitution, physical compression strengthens the magnetic exchange by directly shortening bond lengths, providing a new way to engineer magnetism in iridates.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the significant enhancement of the Curie temperature (Tc) and the reduction of the energy gap in the 5d-3d double perovskite Y2NiIrO6 under physical pressure.
main_result: Physical pressure up to 17 GPa increases Tc from 192 K to 240 K and decreases the band gap from 0.36 eV to 0.26 eV by shortening Ni-O and Ir-O bonds, strengthening the AFM superexchange pathway.
method: High-pressure experiments using Diamond Anvil Cells (DAC) combined with in-situ synchrotron XRD, Raman spectroscopy, and magnetization/resistance measurements, supported by DFT+U calculations.
model_or_system: The system is Y2NiIrO6, a 5d-3/d double perovskite with a rock-salt ordered B-site and a J_eff = 1/2 Mott-insulating state, featuring Ni-O-Ir superexchange pathways.
key_observables: Curie temperature (Tc), energy gap (Eg), lattice constants (a, b, c), Ni-O and Ir-O bond lengths, Ni-O-Ir bond angles, and Raman active modes.
important_parameters: Pressure up to 17-35 GPa, Hubbard U values (Ni 3d: 5.0 eV, Ir 5d: 3.0 eV), and the exchange parameter J_Ni-Ir.
main_assumptions: The use of the GGA+U approximation to account for electron correlation in Ni-3d and Ir-5d shells.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows crystal/magnetic structure and XRD patterns; Figure S1-S3 show XRD and Raman spectra under pressure; Figure S4 shows calculated bond lengths, angles, and partial density of states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 5d-3d superexchange problem, describes the structural and magnetic characterization under high pressure, compares physical vs. chemical compression, and provides a DFT-based mechanistic explanation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
